summary: Gruber highlights a peculiar TCM special where Warren Beatty appears both as himself and in character as Dick Tracy, ostensibly to retain the film/TV rights to the character. He argues that Beatty's 1990 Dick Tracy film, while a pop cultural sensation at the time, failed to achieve lasting relevance because of its excessive campiness — unlike Tim Burton's Batman, which remains beloved. Gruber reads the special as Beatty expressing genuine regret about the tone of his film and yearning for a grittier, more grounded approach akin to Nolan's Batman or Craig's Bond. He speculates that Beatty, at 85, may be extending the rights not just to squat on them but as a pitch to actually make a new, darker Dick Tracy film — possibly as director rather than star.
key_points: Warren Beatty produced a TCM special playing both himself and Dick Tracy, likely to retain character rights for another 14 years
Beatty's 1990 Dick Tracy was a star-packed cultural sensation but failed to achieve the lasting beloved status of Burton's Batman
In the special, Beatty praises the grittiness of older Dick Tracy adaptations, signaling regret about his film's campiness
Gruber draws a distinction between films that think real and films that feel real, citing Nolan's Batman and Craig's Bond as models for what a rebooted Dick Tracy could be
The footnote reveals Walter Hill nearly directed a 'violent and realistic' Dick Tracy in the mid-80s — a tantalizing what-if
money_quotes: It is not cinema, by any stretch, but it is absolutely worth watching.
Dick Tracy, on the other hand, is, if not forgotten, no longer culturally relevant.
A cameo from Beatty as Dick Tracy in a blockbuster movie trailer today would have most people asking 'Who's he?'
Beatty clearly regrets the film's campiness. He knows he swung and missed at a chance for a classic.
Sometimes the truth of a thing isn't in the think of it, but in the feel of it.
Nolan's Batman, Daniel Craig's Bond, and Tom Cruise's Mission Impossible — they feel real in some ways despite not thinking real at all.
Why else do this — why extend the rights for another 14 years when he's 85 years old?
Or maybe it's just one hell of an interesting goof.
key_insight: Beatty's quirky rights-retention special reveals a deeper regret about choosing camp over grit, and possibly a late-career ambition to get Dick Tracy right by embracing the grounded, emotionally authentic approach that made Nolan's Batman and Craig's Bond endure.
